item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd kind of like to see what would happen if a foundation agent fully versed in what they know about it was moved there.

Would they be able to remember it from the outside or would they deny knowledge of containment info on it?

Also wonder what would happen if they legally changed the 1075s address.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand why it was necessary, or even desirable, to sour public opinion.

The anomaly is belief in and memories of the forests personhood, which remain just as strong and numerous whether the populace likes him or not.
The frame job runs the risk of antagonizing an at least pseudo-sentient skip, the second interview even hints at that possibility. If she had been a little more receptive to Vincent's "paranoia', she might have actually stumbled onto something and caused a secrecy breach.
By its very nature the assault reports left a public paper trail that may provide clues if anyone else forms new memories of Vincent "ranting" about persecution.

Alternate containment suggestion: Buy up the town, or fake a toxic waste spill, or any of several other means to force all residents to live somewhere outside the line where the memory effect applies. Then establish legal residences for a small handful of either staff or D-Class inside the town.
Voila! Full containment with no civilian exposure to an open-ended mind-screwing anomaly.
With added bonus of easier monitoring for new memories as they are generated; because you can just openly ask the folks you moved in without fabricating a cover story about why you want to know what Vincent is up to.

Obligatory alternative interpretation: What if Vincent is a real boy? The anomaly is actually that everyone outside of townsfolk can only perceive him as a grove of trees. Legal residents are immune because of a technicality in the phrasing of the gypsy woman's curse. But Foundation staff can't figure it out because the mind-warp won't let them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is it the trees or the location itself which the SCP is?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: What about other trees-as-people based links? Deci mentions SCP-1500 upthread, and SCP-1710 seems to be what this initially appears that it might be but isn't. Although I am not really sure how to make a sensible link in either case.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'm not a huge fan of creating an addendum that is completely unrelated to the narrative of the piece. I'm not sure of what may work here but this doesn't work for me. I know I said so in chat but I'm mentioning it here as well in case I don't get back to this in the next 5 days or so.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: In addition to the aforementioned, SCP-2478 is also a delusion-based anomaly which causes people of certain demographic traits (ethnic Japanese, in this case) to perceive those instances as humans of large sizes (and not bat an eyelid to it).

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. No edits made per author request. See Discussion below.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Between this article, SCP-2784, and SCP-1679, mass delusion causing people to perceive individuals in states different than what they are in reality is a pretty common anomalous effect. Each of these articles, however, goes about the effect in a different way, and on different scales. Still its not unreasonable to investigate whether or not all three of these phenomena are tapping into the same anomalous source. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Adding the following paragraph after the document collapsibles:

Similarities between the mass delusion effects observed in SCP-1075, and those documented in SCP-2784 and SCP-1679 have been noted. Research into whether these three phenomena share a similar origin, or variants of one another is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done, +1

I love how there's the possibility that Vince is a real being, in which case the Foundation have ruined his life, and for no good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for, among other things, the wordplay in the title, the fact that the colony of trees is an organ donor, and for making me feel sympathy for that colony of trees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feex.

Also thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a couple of "Pagos Springs" in there that I'm pretty sure are unintentional, but I'll leave it up to the author to fix them, in case they are supposed to be there.

First paragraph of the description:

Populus tremuloides (and which are designated SCP-1075-1) is a human resident of Pagos Springs, Colorado.

and second paragraph

well respected member of the Pagos Springs community.

Otherwise, I like it. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I enjoyed this story and found the drivers license to be quite funny.

2. I like the fact that I feel upset that the Foundation is falsely accusing trees of assaulting them.

3. I would be interested to know what a Foundation interview with -1 would look like if one personnel temporarily changed their address. Presumably they would know they were talking to trees, but would be incapable of not acknowledging an imaginary person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is what happens when local legends take on a life of their own.

Today it's a cute, if apocryphal story; tomorrow it's getting drunk and beating up the locals.

Ups.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good one. I found this to be thought provoking, and I enjoyed the interview. A SCP is supposed to be thought provoking, so you did alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a novel idea that I enjoyed reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Site-13, Containment Branch

"All right, Jenkins, next on the list is the tree guy. Whaddaya got for me?"

"Well, sir, our plan is to make all affected persons in the town hate it."

"Hate it?"

"We will manufacture a series of false assault charges to alter public opinion—"

"English, Jenkins!"

"…We're going to tell everyone the tree guy beat us up."

"Gads, that's devilish! Jenkins, I could kiss you right now!"

"That won't be necessary, sir."

Part of me wonders why this is an SCP. I mean, it's just a town full of people thinking crazy thoughts. The only real anomaly is that you can manipulate those thoughts by changing their addresses. But that part is less than half of me, so you're getting an upvote, because this is quirky and charming and I like it, Zachary Callahan analogues be damned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the kid was Joshua… Tree?

Even if that retched little pun was on purpose, I absolutely love this article. What a strange idea. Articles like this one are why I love this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. That's when the clonal colony was first established (at least in the reality where this is just a bunch of trees). Clonal colonies live for a very very long time. This one, for example, is estimated to be 80,000 years old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the solution to the problem. Feels like something straight out of hitchhikers.

That being said.

A birth certificate dated to February 10th in the year 201 AD.

Surely this is unintentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that does it for me :)

I love Community, but it's not exactly what I need on my brain when reading weird fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should indeed, assuming that I understood the timeline of the story correctly (baby was probably born in the '99-2000 range?).

Happy to help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I switched the one mention of Aurora to Denver, from what you say, that should correct the issue? Also thanks, I try to get the little details exactly right so I'd be a little irked if that stayed wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor factual note: the University of Colorado Hospital in Aurora didn't open until 2007.

The Fitzsimons Army Medical Center (only a couple blocks away) didn't close down until mid-1999, so that could conceivably fit into your timeline. Alternatively, the "old" University Hospital was in Denver until the new one opened. Either one would work.

It's a minor nitpick, but since I'm pretty familiar with their history it kinda pulls me out of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple concept and thus a story driven scp, pulled off with flying colors. I like it, even if it doesn't do anything that hasn't been done before, it does it well enough that I do not care. It's solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never tought an article could make me feel sorry for a bunch of trees.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like Zachary Callahan has a brother now!

That's my biggest issue with this, finely written as it is; reminds me way too much of that article for some reason to really enjoy or get into. The effect is wearing a bit thin but that connection to 1500 in particular is what bothers me enough to not upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are you telling me the DMV can't even get imaginary tree people's licenses right? I mean, goddamn.

Note: It wasn't intentional but is now a feature. Also good eyes because even when knowing what to look for I had to look twice to find it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was his last name supposed to be mis-spelt on the driver's licence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't feeling it at first, but that last interview really sold it for me. You actually managed to make me feel bad for an imaginary tree man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One reference too far eh? If I kicked the name would that change your vote?

Edit: I just switched it from "community" to "city". Still a reference, just less of one and maybe less immersion breaking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Greendale Community College…. -_-

I enjoyed the article generally. "Aspenson" and the documents were quite amusing. I felt the appropriate sadness at the nature of the cover-up, and really enjoyed the way the last interview was framed. Nice little small-town paranoia from the view of the people doing the framing.

Greendale kicked me right out of the article, though. Killed my suspension of disbelief. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used that template as a reference (stuff like the mountain background or the placement of information), but it's completely different because I didn't feel like creating a forgery. Also this was easier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Colorado driver's license is bugging me because I found this template online and it looked almost completely different from the one in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

It is always a pleasure to see a skip that I think would make even the most seasoned, veteran Foundation researchers just throw up their hands and say, "okay, what the fuck???" This was an enthusiastic upvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cubey: They would not. Which prior to containment meant they looked like the crazy ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delightfully weird.

The only thing that bothers me about this article is the way the effect propagates. Just changing your legal address is enough for the SCP to start or stop affecting you? Would someone who lives in Pagosa Springs on a permanent basis, but is not officially registered as a resident (due to overlook, being homeless, or whatever else) be affected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image Credits:

First image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:PAGOSA_HOT_SPRINGS,_COLORADO_-_NARA_-_524217.jpg

Organ Donor Heart: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hj%C3%A4rta.gif

ID Photo: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Populus_tremuloides_4345.JPG

ID background: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:IPW.JPG

Photo ID was created from the above elements and a bit of my own stuff in MSpaint by me.

Special thanks to WrongJohnSilver, Aleph, GreenWolf, AugmentedSpartan, MinMin, and anyone else I'm missing who looked this over as a draft.
